   514→PersonalLLM: Tailoring LLMs to Individual Preferences
   515→Thomas P. Zollo∗Andrew Wei Tung Siah∗1
   516→Naimeng Ye Ang Li Hongseok Namkoong
   517→Columbia University
   518→{tpz2105, andrew.siah, ny2336, al4263, hongseok.namkoong}@columbia.edu
   519→Abstract
   520→As LLMs become capable of complex tasks, there is growing potential for personalized
   521→interactions tailored to the subtle and idiosyncratic preferences of the user. We present a public
   522→benchmark, PersonalLLM , focusing on adapting LLMs to provide maximal benefits for a particular
   523→user. Departing from existing alignment benchmarks that implicitly assume uniform preferences,
   524→we curate open-ended prompts paired with many high-quality answers over which users would be
   525→expected to display heterogeneous latent preferences. Instead of persona-prompting LLMs based
   526→onhigh-levelattributes(e.g., user’sraceorresponselength), whichyieldshomogeneouspreferences
   527→relative to humans, we develop a method that can simulate a large user base with diverse
   528→preferences from a set of pre-trained reward models. Our dataset and generated personalities
   529→offer an innovative testbed for developing personalization algorithms that grapple with continual
   530→data sparsity—few relevant feedback from the particular user—by leveraging historical data
   531→from other (similar) users. We explore basic in-context learning and meta-learning baselines to
   532→illustrate the utility of PersonalLLM and highlight the need for future methodological development.
   533→Our dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/namkoong-lab/PersonalLLM .
   534→1 Introduction
   535→Thealignment of LLMs with human preferences has recently received much attention, with a focus
   536→on adapting model outputs to reflect universal population-level values. A typical goal is to take a
   537→pre-trained model that cannot reliably follow complex user instructions [ 40] and can easily be made
   538→to produce dangerous and offensive responses [ 33] and adapt it to the instructions of its user base
   539→[30] or train a generally helpful and harmless assistant [ 2]. By assuming a uniform preference across
   540→the population, recent successes [ 45,30,7] demonstrate the feasibility of learning and optimizing a
   541→monolithic preference (“reward model”). Alignment techniques have provided the basis for popular
   542→commercial applications like ChatGPT, as well as instruction-tuned open-source models [38].
   543→The rapid advancement in LLM capabilities opens the door to an even more refined notion of
   544→human preference alignment: personalization. A personalized model should adapt to the preferences
   545→and needs of a particular user, and provide maximal benefits as it accumulates interactions (see
   546→Figure 1). Given the expected data sparsity in this setting, beyond a particular user’s data, such
   547→personalized language systems will likely also rely on historical data from other (similar) users in
   548→order to learn how to learn from a small set of new user feedback (see Figure 2). By discovering
   549→patterns across users, these systems will be able to efficiently optimize t
   550→
   551→--- PAGE 2 ---
   552→Figure 1: Standard LLMs require tedious re-prompting to learn a user’s preferences in each session.
   553→PersonalLLM aims to learn a unique user’s diverse preferences to maximize long-term satisfaction.
   554→•Personalized learning experiences could be crafted by adapting educational chat assistants
   555→to the specific learning pace and style of individual students based on previous successful
   556→interactions with similar students.
   557→•Customer support chatbots could offer more accurate and empathetic responses by drawing
   558→on a wealth of previous interactions, leading to quicker resolution times and higher customer
   559→satisfaction.
   560→•In healthcare, personalized chatbots could provide tailored advice based on patients with
   561→similar medical histories and communication preferences.
   562→Compared to conventional applications where prompts have a uniform notion of “ground truth”
   563→(e.g., question answering), LLM personalization is distinguished by the need to study open-ended
   564→prompts where users exhibit heterogeneous preferences across many possible high-quality answers
   565→(Figure 1). While personal preferences may vary according to simple features like user age [ 6,5] and
   566→answer length and technicality [ 24], they also involve more abstract dimensions of culture, politics,
   567→and language [ 19], as well as aspects of personality that are difficult to explain [ 16]. A personalized
   568→LLM should be able to adapt to subtle, idiosyncratic, and sometimes sensitive differences between
   569→user tastes as it gathers more interactions.
   570→Inspired by the vision of a future with personalized AI, we introduce PersonalLLM , a public,
   571→open-source benchmark designed to adapt LLMs to provide maximal benefits for individual users.
   572→In order to explore complex differences in user tastes, our benchmark features a set of prompts with
   573→many high-quality LLM responses (from state-of-the-art LLMs like GPT-4o, Claude 3 Opus, and
   574→Gemini 1.5 Pro), such that humans are expected to express diverse preferences over the responses.
   575→Such an approach to dataset-building stands in contrast to existing alignment datasets, where
   576→responses exhibit observable quality differences (see Figure 3).
   577→2
   578→
   579→--- PAGE 3 ---
   580→For each prompt and set of responses, our dataset also includes scores from a set of 10 reward
   581→models with heterogeneous preferences over those responses. We leverage these reward models to
   582→sample many new “users” (or personal preference models) via weighted ensembles of their preferences,
   583→and in doing so we are able to simulate an entire user base , which we argue to be a critical ingredient
   584→in a truly useful personalization benchmark. Through extensive analysis of the preferences of these
   585→users over our dataset, we show these simulated personal preference models to be diverse and
   586→non-trivial (e.g., with respect to length, formatting, or tone). We illustrate the difficulty of creating
   587→such an environment by comparing to the increasingly popular persona prompting baseline [ 5,6,18],
   588→which in our analysis produces preferences only half as diverse as a set of PersonalLLM users across
   589→multiple metrics. Taken together, the prompts, responses, and personalities present in PersonalLLM
   590→offer an innovative tested for benchmarking personalization algorithms as they tailor interactions
   591→based on previous interactions with an individual user.
   592→While fine-tuning and reinforcement learning approaches [ 37,34] are effective for aligning to
   593→population-level preferences, personalization requires a new algorithmic toolkit, as it is not practical
   594→to gather enough data or store a separate copy of the model or even low-rank adapter weights [ 15] for
   595→every user. PersonalLLM offers the versatility necessary to spur development across a range of new
   596→approaches to personalization: in-context learning (ICL) [ 4], retrieval augmented generation (RAG)
   597→[22], ranking agents, efficient fine-tuning, and other adaptation techniques. In our experiments,
   598→we highlight a particularly salient challenge compared to typical applications of personalization
   599→in recommendation systems: the space of “actions/responses” is prohibitively large to be able to
   600→explore based on interactions on a single user. Since this necessitates learning across users , we model
   601→this as a meta-learning problem where the goal is to leverage a wealth of prior interactions from
   602→historical users to tailor responses for a new user who do not have a significant interaction history
   603→(see Figure 2).
   604→Motivated by key methodological gaps in personalizing LLMs, here we summarize our contribu-
   605→tions:
   606→•We release a new open-source dataset with over 10K open-ended prompts paired with 8
   607→high-quality responses from top LLMs.
   608→•We propose a novel method for sampling “users” (i.e., personal preference models) that, unlike
   609→existing methods, creates diverse preferences and allows for the simulation of large historical
   610→user bases.
   611→•We illustrate new possibilities for algorithmic development in learning acrossusers.
   612→Our goal in creating the open-source PersonalLLM testbed is to facilitate work on methods
   613→to personalize the output of an LLM to the individual tastes of many diverse users. We do not
   614→claim our simulated personal preference

   621→arXiv:2403.05006v1  [cs.LG]  8 Mar 2024Provable Multi-Party Reinforcement Learning
   622→with Diverse Human Feedback
   623→Huiying Zhong∗Zhun Deng†Weijie J. Su‡Zhiwei Steven Wu§Linjun Zhang¶
   624→Abstract
   625→Reinforcement learning with human feedback (RLHF) is an eme rging paradigm to align models with human
   626→preferences. Typically, RLHF aggregates preferences from multiple individuals who have diverse viewpoints that
   627→may conﬂict with each other. Our work initiates the theoretical study of multi-party RLHF that explicitly
   628→models the diverse preferences of multiple individuals. We show how traditional RLHF approaches can fail
   629→since learning a single reward function cannot capture and b alance the preferences of multiple individuals. To
   630→overcome such limitations, we incorporate meta-learning t o learn multiple preferences and adopt diﬀerent social
   631→welfare functions to aggregate the preferences across mult iple parties. We focus on the oﬄine learning setting and
   632→establish sample complexity bounds, along with eﬃciency an d fairness guarantees, for optimizing diverse social
   633→welfare functions suchas Nash, Utilitarian, andLeximinwe lfare functions. Ourresults showaseparation between
   634→the sample complexities of multi-party RLHF and traditiona l single-party RLHF. Furthermore, we consider a
   635→reward-free setting, where each individual’s preference i s no longer consistent with a reward model, and give
   636→pessimistic variants of the von Neumann Winner based on oﬄin e preference data. Taken together, our work
   637→showcases the advantage of multi-party RLHF but also highli ghts its more demanding statistical complexity.
   638→1 Introduction
   639→Recent advancements in AI alignment focus on training AI systems t o align with user preferences. A prevalent
   640→strategy involves integrating human feedback into the learning pro cess, a technique that has signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced
   641→the development of language models and reinforcement learning amo ng other areas ( Ouyang et al. ,2022;Ziegler
   642→et al.,2019). Established RLHF methods typically ﬁt a reward model over users ’ preferences data (in the form of
   643→pairwise comparisons) and then train policies to optimize the learned r eward. The implicit assumption behind this
   644→approach is that diﬀerent users preferences can be modeled via a s ingle reward function.
   645→However, this type of single-party reward-based assumption is at odds with the fact that users may p ossess
   646→heterogeneous viewpoints that conﬂict with each other. For exam ple, the nature of human opinion in open-ended
   647→social decision-making scenarios is inherently diverse and dynamic, m aking traditional reinforcement learning from

   657→single-party RLHF can fail to balance multiple individuals’ preference s, consider the following toy example.
   658→Example (2 parties and 3 alternatives). Consider a scenario where users have preferences over three op tions
   659→A,B,C. Halfofthepopulationprefers A≻C≻B(withunderlyingrewardsof R1(A) = 1,R1(C) = 1−ǫ,R1(B) = 0

   661→1−ǫ,R2(A) = 0). Then pooling both sub-populations’ preferences together , we cannot diﬀerentiate between A,B,C
   662→via pairwise comparison. Thus, if we learn a single reward function Rover the pooled pairwise comparison data,
   663→the resulting Rwill have R(A) =R(B) =R(C). This leads to a policy that selects the three options uniformly at
   664→random, which has an average reward less than 2 /3. However, if we learn each sub-population’s reward separately,
   665→we can potentially arrive at a better policy that only recommends opt ionBand achieves an average reward of 1 −ǫ.

   668→How can models be trained to align with the preferences of het erogeneous individuals?
   669→Our work takes a step towards addressing this question by initiating a theoretical exploration of multi-party RLHF,
   670→that aims to explicitly model and balance diverse heterogeneous pre ferences from diﬀerent individuals. We take
   671→inspiration from social choice theory ( Jin et al. ,2020;Noothigattu et al. ,2020), which oﬀers an extensive array of
   672→toolsforaggregatinghumanpreferences. Atahighlevel, weexten dtheframeworkofRLHFtodirectlyaccommodate

   675→and later generalize to the Markov Decision Process (MDP). Our prim ary focus is on the popular oﬄine setting,
   676→where the dataset is pre-collected. The insights of our approache s can be generalized to a wider range of scenarios.
   677→Notably, recent works by Zhu et al. (2023) andZhan et al. (2023) provided an initial theoretical analysis of oﬄine
   678→RLHF. Our paper takes a step further by considering cases with mu ltiple parties and heterogeneous preferences.

   688→The novelty of our work lies in two aspects. First, we employ a meta-le arning technique for learning multiple
   689→reward functions from limited observations, by utilizing a common fea ture representation among various parties.
   690→This strategy enhances learning eﬃciency in environments where da ta are scarce, drawing on the foundational work
   691→in few-shot learning ( Du et al. ,2020;Tripuraneni et al. ,2020). Second, we integrate a pessimistic approach within
   692→social welfare functions to guarantee model invariance with the inc lusion of zero rewards in the presence of multiple
   693→parties. This aspect of our work extends the principles established byZhu et al. (2023), where we establish a
   694→sub-optimality bound for the (Nash’s) social welfare function. This bound is derived by aggregating heterogeneous
   695→individual rewards, necessitating a more stringent data coverage condition than what is typically required in RLHF
   696→scenarios involving a single entity.
   697→2 Related Work
   698→Meta-learning Meta-learning, or learning to learn, seeks to design a learner to quic kly learn new tasks based on
   699→a few prior tasks. Some of the dominant approaches learn a common representation among multiple tasks, referred
   700→to as representation learning ( Baxter,2000;Finn et al. ,2017;Ji et al.,2023;Maurer et al. ,2016). Theoretical
   701→guarantees for multi-task linear regression with low-dimensional line ar representation have been established in the
   702→literature ( Du et al. ,2020;Li and Zhang ,2023;Tripuraneni et al. ,2020). Our work contributes to generalizing
   703→this line of work, by investigating the learning-to-learn abilities in huma n preference-based models with a more
   704→complicated structure.
   705→Reinforcement Learning with Human Feedback Human preference is widely used in RL since preference
   706→comparison is easier to elicit than numerical reward ( Chen et al. ,2022;Ouyang et al. ,2022;Ziegler et al. ,2019).
   707→Particularly, the most related works to ours are Zhu et al. (2023) andZhan et al. (2023), both of which study oﬄine
   708→RLHF with areward-basedpreferencemodel. Zhuet al. (2023)focusedonaBradley-Terry-Luce(BTL)model under
   709→a linear reward function, while Zhan et al. (2023) further considered general function classes. Lastly, we highligh t
   710→the latest work by Wang et al. (2023), which established two preference models akin to ours, but in the c ontext
   711→of traditional single-party RLHF in online learning. In contrast, we c onsider a broader setting with heterogeneous
   712→individuals. Particularly, oﬄine RL is more challenging than online RL due t o limited data availability. Thus,
   713→the pessimistic technique has been widely studied in recent years, as witnessed in CBs ( Li et al.,2022), MDPs
   714→(Xie et al. ,2021), and Markov games ( Cui and Du ,2022). In this paper, we also utilize pessimism to address the
   715→coverage challenges.

   724→Few-shot Steerable Alignment:
   725→Adapting Rewards and LLM Policies with Neural Processes
   726→Katarzyna Kobalczyk* 1Claudio Fanconi* 1Hao Sun1Mihaela van der Schaar1
   727→Abstract
   728→As large language models (LLMs) become in-
   729→creasingly embedded in everyday applications,
   730→ensuring their alignment with the diverse prefer-
   731→ences of individual users has become a critical
   732→challenge. Currently deployed approaches typi-
   733→cally assume homogeneous user objectives and
   734→rely on single-objective fine-tuning. However, hu-
   735→man preferences are inherently heterogeneous, in-
   736→fluenced by various unobservable factors, leading
   737→to conflicting signals in preference data. Exist-
   738→ing solutions addressing this diversity often re-
   739→quire costly datasets labelled for specific objec-
   740→tives and involve training multiple reward models
   741→or LLM policies, which is computationally expen-
   742→sive and impractical. In this work, we present a
   743→novel framework for few-shot steerable alignment,
   744→where users’ underlying preferences are inferred
   745→from a small sample of their choices. To achieve
   746→this, we extend the Bradley-Terry-Luce model
   747→to handle heterogeneous preferences with unob-
   748→served variability factors and propose its practical
   749→implementation for reward modelling and LLM
   750→fine-tuning. Thanks to our proposed approach of
   751→functional parameter-space conditioning, LLMs
   752→trained with our framework can be adapted to
   753→individual preferences at inference time, gener-
   754→ating outputs over a continuum of behavioural
   755→modes. We empirically validate the effectiveness
   756→of methods, demonstrating their ability to capture
   757→and align with diverse human preferences in a
   758→data-efficient manner. Our code is made available
   759→at: https://github.com/kasia-kobalczyk/few-shot-
   760→steerable-alignment.
   761→*Equal contribution1Department of Applied Mathematics
   762→and Theoretical Physics, University of Cambridge, Cambridge,
   763→United Kingdom. Correspondence to: Katarzyna Kobalczyk
   764→<knk25@cam.ac.uk >, Claudio Fanconi <caf83@cam.ac.uk >.
   765→Preprint.1. Introduction
   766→Motivation: The need for personalisation. As large lan-
   767→guage models (LLMs) become more integrated into daily
   768→life, it is critical that these models align with diverse human
   769→values and preferences. Current methods for LLM training,
   770→such as Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
   771→(RLHF) and Direct Preference optimisation (DPO), rely on
   772→single-objective fine-tuning, which assumes uniformity in
   773→human preferences. In reality, users of LLM-based sys-
   774→tems often have diverse and even conflicting goals, influ-
   775→enced by factors like demographic and cultural backgrounds,
   776→cognitive processes, or implicit ambiguity in their prompt
   777→messages. These factors are often unobservable, leading to
   778→preference data that contains conflicting signals. When such
   779→data is aggregated under a homogeneous Bradley-Terry-
   780→Luce (BTL) model (Bradley & Terry, 1952), the result is
   781→equivalent to a Borda count (Siththaranjan et al., 2024),
   782→which fails to capture the true diversity in human prefer-
   783→ences.
   784→Challenges: Unknown sources of 
   785→
   786→--- PAGE 2 ---
   787→Few-shot Steerable Alignment: Adapting Rewards and LLM Policies with Neural Processes
   788→on a dataset of heterogeneous preference choices without re-
   789→lying on predefined objectives or requiring training multiple
   790→independent reward or language models.
   791→Contributions: ▶We extend the standard BTL model to
   792→account for the heterogeneous preferences across a popula-
   793→tion of users, enabling few-shot adaptation to individualised
   794→reward functions. ▶By modelling user-level reward func-
   795→tions as samples from a stochastic process, we propose a
   796→practical implementation of this model based on Neural Pro-
   797→cesses (NPs) (Garnelo et al., 2018a;b). We demonstrate its
   798→applicability in both reward modelling ( NP-BTL ) and di-
   799→rect preference optimisation ( NP-DPO ).▶By introducing
   800→functional, parameter-space conditioning, NP-DPO opens
   801→up the possibility for training LLMs that be adapted to indi-
   802→vidual users’ preferences at inference time and can generate
   803→outputs across a continuum of behavioural modes, reflecting
   804→diverse human preferences without constraining the factors
   805→of variability to a finite set of fixed, pre-defined objectives
   806→nor requiring training and storage of multiple models. ▶We
   807→empirically evaluate NP-BTL and NP-DPO, demonstrating
   808→and critically analysing their effectiveness in capturing and
   809→aligning with heterogeneous human preferences.

   877→3. Method
   878→The following section outlines our approach to addressing
   879→the problem of few-shot steerable alignment defined above.
   880→First, in section 3.1, we extend the BTL reward model to
   881→capture user-specific preferences by conditioning on latent
   882→variables inferred from the few-shot sample of user-specific
   883→contextual data points. We then propose a practical imple-
   884→mentation of this model based on Neural Processes (Garnelo
   885→et al., 2018a;b)–a class of meta-learning models enabling to
   886→infer the posterior predictive distribution over the outcomes
   887→in a fully amortised manner. We refer to this conditional
   888→reward model as NP-BTL. Given our conditional user pref-
   889→erence model, we apply it in the context of LLM policy
   890→fine-tuning. In section 3.2, we introduce NP-DPO. By ex-
   891→tending the conditional setup to DPO, we bypass explicit
   892→reward model training and propose a method for directly
   893→optimising LLMs with respect to the heterogeneous mixture
   894→of user preferences data.

   943→Controllable and explainable personality sliders for LLMs at inference time
   944→Florian Hoppe1 2David Khachaturov2Robert Mullins2Mark Huasong Meng1 3
   945→Abstract
   946→Aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with
   947→specific personas typically relies on expensive
   948→and monolithic Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) or
   949→RLHF. While effective, these methods require
   950→training distinct models for every target person-
   951→ality profile. Inference-time activation steering
   952→offers a parameter-efficient alternative, yet naive
   953→approaches fail to control multiple traits simulta-
   954→neously due to destructive vector interference. In
   955→this work, we propose a modular framework for
   956→continuous, multi-dimensional personality con-
   957→trol. Our key innovation isSequential Adaptive
   958→Steering (SAS): a method that orthogonalizes
   959→steering vectors by training subsequent probes on
   960→the residual stream shifted by prior interventions.
   961→This approach transforms steering vectors into
   962→reusable primitives, allowing users to instantly
   963→synthesize complex, high-fidelity personality pro-
   964→files by simply adjusting coefficients ( α). We
   965→validate our framework on the Big Five personal-
   966→ity traits, demonstrating that it outperforms naive
   967→baselines in both goal adherence and coherence,
   968→enabling precise, holistic personality modulation
   969→without updating model parameters.
   970→1. Introduction
   971→Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated excep-
   972→tional generalization across diverse tasks, yet real-world
   973→applications often demand specific, consistent personas —
   974→from empathetic therapy assistants and engaging roleplay
   975→characters to objective customer support agents (Li et al.,
   976→2025b). While users can prompt models to adopt these roles,
   977→prompt engineering remains fragile: models frequently ex-
   978→hibit contextual drift within long context windows, and com-
   979→plex persona instructions consume valuable token budgets
   980→(Kaplan et al., 2020).
   981→Existing alternatives for durable alignment, such as Super-
   982→1Technical University of Munich, Germany2University of Cam-
   983→bridge, United Kingdom3University College Dublin, Ireland. Cor-
   984→respondence to: Florian Hoppe<f.hoppe@tum.de>.
   985→Preprint. March 5, 2026.vised Fine-Tuning (SFT) or Direct Preference Optimization
   986→(DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2023; Ziegler et al., 2019), are ef-
   987→fective but monolithic. Training a separate model for every
   988→desired combination of personality traits is computationally
   989→prohibitive (Ilharco et al., 2023). For instance, a user requir-
   990→ing a persona that is both “highly extraverted” and “highly
   991→conscientious” cannot simply compose a model fine-tuned
   992→for extraversion with one fine-tuned for conscientiousness
   993→(Aghajanyan et al., 2021). This lack of modularity leads to
   994→a combinatorial explosion: supporting all possible combi-
   995→nations of Ndistinct traits would require training 2Nsepa-
   996→rate models. While recent approaches like “LoRA Soups”
   997→propose merging low-rank adapters to enable modular skill
   998→composition without full re-training (Prabhakar et al., 2024),
   999→these methods still rely on updating and

  1011→forms naive baselines.Our contributions are as follows:
  1012→•Sequential Adaptive Steering (SAS):We introduce a
  1013→novel framework that enables the composition of mul-
  1014→tiple personality traits at inference time. By training
  1015→probes on activation distributions shifted by prior in-
  1016→terventions, SAS effectively orthogonalizes steering
  1017→vectors and mitigates the destructive interference ob-
  1018→served in naive approaches.
  1019→•Automated Layer Selection:We propose a data-
  1020→driven method using the Fisher Ratio to automatically
  1021→identify the optimal intervention layers for specific se-
  1022→mantic traits, replacing heuristic trial-and-error with a
  1023→quantifiable separability metric.
  1024→•Empirical Validation:We demonstrate that our frame-
  1025→work achieves Pareto dominance over baselines in the
  1026→trade-off between goal adherence and model perplex-
  1027→ity. We validate these results across Llama-3, Mistral,
  1028→and Qwen architectures, confirming the linearity of
  1029→personality representations in large language models.
  1030→2. Related Work
  1031→2.1. Alignment & Fine-Tuning
  1032→Aligning Language Models (LLMs) to specific behav-
  1033→iors has traditionally relied on Supervised Fine-Tuning
  1034→(SFT) or Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback
  1035→(RLHF). More recently, Direct Preference Optimization
  1036→(DPO) (Rafailov et al., 2023) has emerged as a stable al-
  1037→ternative, implicitly optimizing the policy without a sepa-
  1038→rate reward model. While effective, these methods produce
  1039→monolithic models: a model tuned for “High Extraversion”
  1040→cannot easily be combined with one tuned for “High Consci-
  1041→entiousness” without retraining on a mixed dataset (Ziegler
  1042→et al., 2019).
  1043→Recent work onTask Arithmetic(Ilharco et al., 2023) at-
  1044→tempts to merge fine-tuned weights (e.g. θnew=θbase+
  1045→λ(θft−θbase)). However, weight merging often degrades
  1046→performance when tasks are conflicting or when the under-
  1047→lying optimization trajectories diverge (Aghajanyan et al.,
  1048→2021). In contrast, our activation-based approach avoids
  1049→expensive weight modification entirely, enabling modular,
  1050→inference-time composition of traits.2.2. Inference-Time Activation Steering
  1051→Activation steering intervenes directly on the internal repre-
  1052→sentations of the model during the forward pass. Early work
  1053→by Turner et al. (2023) and Zou et al. (2023) demonstrated
  1054→that adding a fixed “steering vector” to residual stream acti-
  1055→vations can robustly elicit simple behaviors (e.g., maintain-
  1056→ing a sentiment or topic).
  1057→Specifically for personality, Chen et al. (2023) introduced
  1058→“Persona Vectors” to monitors

  1108→Despite this sequential application, naive multi-vector steer-

  1111→Once an earlier probe is activated (e.g., α1̸= 0), it induces
  1112→a distributional shift in downstream representations. Subse-
  1113→quent probes, having never observed this shifted activation
  1114→manifold during training, may no longer align with the se-
  1115→mantic direction of their target traits. This mismatch leads
  1116→to destructive interference, manifesting as reduced goal ad-
  1117→herence and degraded generation coherence.3.4. Sequential Adaptive Steering
  1118→To resolve this, we proposeSequential Adaptive Steering.
  1119→We construct probes sequentially, ensuring that each new
  1120→probe is robust to the shifts induced by prior probes.
  1121→As illustrated in Figure 2, we train each subsequent probe on
  1122→a composite dataset containing both unsteered activations
  1123→and activations shifted by varying intensities of predecessor
  1124→probes. During training, we sample αtrain uniformly from
  1125→a stable range (the selection of which is explained in Sec-
  1126→tion 3.7) to simulate the distributional shifts induced by prior
  1127→interventions. This exposure forces the probe to learn a di-
  1128→rection that is invariant to these perturbations — effectively
  1129→orthogonalizing the new vector relative to the subspaces
  1130→spanned by previous traits and ensuring robustness against
  1131→interference.

  1144→Personalized Steering of Large Language Models:
  1145→Versatile Steering Vectors Through Bi-directional
  1146→Preference Optimization
  1147→Yuanpu Cao, Tianrong Zhang, Bochuan Cao, Ziyi Yin,
  1148→Lu Lin, Fenglong Ma, Jinghui Chen
  1149→The Pennsylvania State University
  1150→{ymc5533, tbz5156, bccao, zmy5171, lulin, fenglong, jzc5917}@psu.edu
  1151→Abstract
  1152→Researchers have been studying approaches to steer the behavior of Large Language
  1153→Models (LLMs) and build personalized LLMs tailored for various applications.
  1154→While fine-tuning seems to be a direct solution, it requires substantial compu-
  1155→tational resources and may significantly affect the utility of the original LLM.
  1156→Recent endeavors have introduced more lightweight strategies, focusing on ex-
  1157→tracting “steering vectors” to guide the model’s output toward desired behaviors
  1158→by adjusting activations within specific layers of the LLM’s transformer architec-
  1159→ture. However, such steering vectors are directly extracted from the activations of
  1160→human preference data and thus often lead to suboptimal results and occasional
  1161→failures, especially in alignment-related scenarios. In this work, we propose an
  1162→innovative approach that could produce more effective steering vectors through
  1163→bi-directional preference optimization. Our method is designed to allow steer-
  1164→ing vectors to directly influence the generation probability of contrastive human
  1165→preference data pairs, thereby offering a more precise representation of the target
  1166→behavior. By carefully adjusting the direction and magnitude of the steering vector,
  1167→we enabled personalized control over the desired behavior across a spectrum of
  1168→intensities. Extensive experimentation across various open-ended generation tasks,
  1169→particularly focusing on steering AI personas, has validated the efficacy of our
  1170→approach. Moreover, we comprehensively investigate critical alignment-concerning
  1171→scenarios, such as managing truthfulness, mitigating hallucination, and addressing
  1172→jailbreaking attacks alongside their respective defenses. Remarkably, our method
  1173→can still demonstrate outstanding steering effectiveness across these scenarios. Fur-
  1174→thermore, we showcase the transferability of our steering vectors across different
  1175→models/LoRAs and highlight the synergistic benefits of applying multiple vectors
  1176→simultaneously. These findings significantly broaden the practicality and versatility
  1177→of our proposed method. Code is available at https://github.com/CaoYuanpu/BiPO
  1178→1 Introduction
  1179→In recent years, the generalization capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) [ 31,20] have
  1180→improved substantially, driven by the increase in parameter size and the expansion of training
  1181→text corpus [ 22,15]. Despite the strong generalization capabilities of LLMs across diverse fields
  1182→[11,30,8,19,13,34,38], researchers are still actively investigating approaches to steer the behaviors
  1183→of LLMs and develop personalized models tailored to meet the specific needs of different users
  1184→[36,32]. While fine-tuning techniques such as supervised fine-
  1185→
  1186→--- PAGE 2 ---
  1187→in the generated text [ 29,32,26,33]. In particular, recent efforts have introduced the extraction
  1188→of “steering vectors” within specific layers of the LLM’s transformer architecture [ 26,33]. During
  1189→inference, these steering vectors are added back to the activations of the original prompt to guide the
  1190→model’s output toward desired behaviors. Rimsky et al. [26] has demonstrated that a steering vector
  1191→corresponding to a specific behavior possesses universal applicability across various user prompts.
  1192→Furthermore, since a single steering vector only influences the activation of a specific layer without
  1193→altering the model weights, it minimally disrupts the original model, thereby preserving the model’s
  1194→general capabilities.
  1195→However, existing steering vectors are typically extracted by the activation difference when the LLM
  1196→is prompted with two opposite preferences [ 26,33], ignoring its actual generation that may diverge
  1197→from the prompt, thus often resulting in suboptimal outcomes and occasional failures, particularly in
  1198→critical alignment-related scenarios. Specifically, such methods first construct a set of contrastive
  1199→prompt pairs, where two prompts in each pair are appended with opposite answers to the same
  1200→question – one answer is associated with the target behavior, while the other corresponds to the
  1201→opposite preference. The steering vector representing the target behavior is then derived as the
  1202→average activation difference of the LLM on all pairs. However, we have observed that the vector
  1203→extracted from prompt pairs has limited steering capability in the model’s generation – the model may
  1204→generate texts that are not aligned with the prompted choice, even when the steering vector is applied
  1205→to each generation step. This discrepancy indicates that steering vectors purely based on contrastive
  1206→prompts may not accurately represent the target generation behavior of the model.
  1207→To fill in this gap, drawing inspiration from the recent preference optimization techniques such as
  1208→Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) [ 25], we introduce an innovative approach to calculate more
  1209→effective steering vectors via Bi-directional Preference Optimization (BiPO ). Instead of relying on the
  1210→model to “follow” a prompted direction, our method allows the model to “speak up”, enabling steering
  1211→vectors to proactively modulate the generation probability of contrastive human preference data pairs,
  1212→thus providing a more precise representation of the target behavior. By further manipulating the
  1213→direction and magnitude of the optimized steering vectors, we can easily achieve varying degrees of
  1214→steering effects for desired behaviors, thereby efficiently meeting users’ diverse personalization needs
  1215→without necessitating additional model training.
  1216→We summarize our contributions as follows:
  1217→•We have analyzed current methods for extracting steering vectors in LLMs, identifying their
  1218→potential limitations and failure cases. Based on these insights, we propose a bi-directional
  1219→prefer

  1222→transformer model. These steering vectors are then applied to the first token position of subsequent
  1223→forward passes to influence model completions. However, it fails to perform consistently across
  1224→various behaviors and prompts. To reduce the noise in the steering vector, contrastive activation
  1225→addition (CAA) [ 26] utilizes hundreds of preference data pairs to generate steering vectors. Each
  1226→pair consists of two prompts with the same multiple-choice question but ending with different
  1227→answer choices. The steering vector corresponding to the target behavior is isolated by averaging
  1228→the difference between the activation on these preference pairs. Their experiments focusing on
  1229→Llama-2 [ 31] have demonstrated CAA can steer AI personas and mitigate hallucinations to some
  1230→extent. Wang and Shu [33] employed a similar technique and additionally tested freeform-format
  1231→contrastive preference data pairs, verifying that steering vectors can be utilized to compromise the
  1232→safety of LLMs. However, these steering vectors are derived directly from the activations of LLMs
  1233→when using preference data pairs as input prompts, which often results in inaccurate representations
  1234→of the target behavior, particularly in alignment-concerning scenarios such as addressing jailbreaking
  1235→attacks and defenses [ 44,24,4,3,37]. Our method, through preference optimization, acquires more
  1236→precise steering vectors and can effectively steer model behaviors even in scenarios highly relevant to
  1237→alignment. In addition to extracting steering vectors in MLP activations of specific layers within the
  1238→transformer’s residual stream, some works opt to add perturbations on attention activations to steer
  1239→model generation [ 14,17]. Focusing on enhancing the truthfulness of LLMs, Li et al. [14] first locates
  1240→a set of attention heads with high probing accuracy for the truthfulness and then shifts activations
  1241→along these truth-correlated directions during inference. Liu et al. [17] improved the efficiency and
  1242→controllability of In-Context Learning [ 2] by substituting traditional demonstration examples with
  1243→shifts in attention activations across all layers of the transformer.
  1244→Preference Optimization Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) has emerged as a
  1245→popular approach for learning from human preference [ 5,42,28,21]. Typically, RLHF first trains
  1246→a neural network-based reward function to harmonize with the preference dataset by incorporating
  1247→a preference model such as the Bradley-Terry model [ 1]. Subsequently, reinforcement learning
  1248→algorithms such as proximal policy optimization [ 27] are adopted to train a language model to
  1249→maximize the given reward for the chosen response. Recent works such as DPO [ 25] and SLiC
  1250→[39,40] have shown the feasibility of circumventing the reward-modeling stage and directly solving
  1251→the actual RL problem, thereby simplifying implementation and reducing resource needs. Specifically,
  1252→DPO [ 25] directly fits a model to human preference data and implicitly optimizes

  1259→SteerX: Disentangled Steering for LLM Personalization
  1260→Xiaoyan Zhao1, Ming Yan1, Yilun Qiu1, Haoting Ni
  1261→Yang Zhang, Fuli Feng, Hong Cheng, Tat-Seng Chua
  1262→xzhao@se.cuhk.edu.hk,{ym689,nhting}@mail.ustc.edu.cn,{qiuyilun,zhangy}@nus.edu.sg
  1263→The Chinese University of Hong Kong, University of Science and Technology of China, National University of Singapore
  1264→Abstract
  1265→Large language models (LLMs) have shown remarkable success
  1266→in recent years, enabling a wide range of applications, including
  1267→intelligent assistants that support users’ daily life and work. A crit-
  1268→ical factor in building such assistants is personalizing LLMs, as
  1269→user preferences and needs vary widely. Activation steering, which
  1270→directly leverages directions representing user preference in the
  1271→LLM activation space to adjust its behavior, offers a cost-effective
  1272→way to align the model’s outputs with individual users. However,
  1273→existing methods rely on all historical data to compute the steer-
  1274→ing vector, ignoring that not all content reflects true user prefer-
  1275→ences, which undermines the personalization signal. To address this,
  1276→we proposeSteerX, a disentangled steering method that isolates
  1277→preference-driven components from preference-agnostic compo-
  1278→nents. Grounded in causal inference theory, SteerX estimates token-
  1279→level causal effects to identify preference-driven tokens, transforms
  1280→these discrete signals into a coherent description, and then lever-
  1281→ages them to steer personalized LLM generation. By focusing on
  1282→the truly preference-driven information, SteerX produces more ac-
  1283→curate activation steering vectors and enhances personalization.
  1284→Experiments on two representative steering backbone methods
  1285→across real-world datasets demonstrate that SteerX consistently
  1286→enhances steering vector quality, offering a practical solution for
  1287→more effective LLM personalization.

  1309→Conference acronym ’XX, June 03–05, 2018, Woodstock, NY Xiaoyan Zhao et al.
  1310→Despite notable progress made by these efforts, we argue that a
  1311→common limitation is that both of their steering vector computa-
  1312→tions rely blindly on all the historical user data, overlooking the fact
  1313→that not all parts of the user data reflect the user’s preferences. Even
  1314→though the textual data is produced by the user, each data piece
  1315→often contains heterogeneous information. Different elements play
  1316→different roles and can naturally show different degrees of relevance
  1317→to user preferences [ 74]. For example, there are some boilerplate
  1318→parts [ 62], e.g., common connecting words or sentences, which
  1319→usually do not reflect user preference but only work as connecting
  1320→functions. Treating all components as equally relevant risks dilutes
  1321→the true preference signal and introduces noise into the steering
  1322→vector, finally preventing the personalization quality.
  1323→In this work, we propose to disentangle preference-driven com-
  1324→ponents from less preference-related ones, focusing on deriving the
  1325→steering vector from the preference-driven components to enable
  1326→more accurate personalization. This task is non-trivial, as there is
  1327→no direct signal indicating which parts of the data are driven by user
  1328→preferences. Inspired by prior studies [ 68,74], we employ a causal
  1329→analysis framework to address this challenge. As illustrated in Fig-
  1330→ure 2, the observed user data arises from the interaction between
  1331→the user and the context. According to causal inference theory,
  1332→determining whether a component is preference-driven requires
  1333→measuring the extent to which it is influenced by the user’s inher-
  1334→ent traits. This influence corresponds to the causal effect of the
  1335→user on the observed data, quantified by the difference between the
  1336→factual world and a counterfactual world where a reference user
  1337→(without individual preference) generates the data. By leveraging
  1338→the magnitude of these causal effects, we can effectively identify
  1339→the preference-driven components of each user data instance.
  1340→To address this, we propose SteerX, a disentangled steering
  1341→method inspired by causal analysis. SteerX follows a disentan-
  1342→gling–smoothing–steering paradigm for LLM personalization: it
  1343→disentangles preference-driven tokens through causal effect estima-
  1344→tion, transforms them into smooth descriptions, and then leverages
  1345→them in the steering process. Specifically, we first estimate token-
  1346→level causal effects: for each historical data instance, we represent
  1347→the user trait using all other histories, then compare the factual
  1348→prediction probabilities of the target history tokens (with the user
  1349→trait) against their counterfactual predictions (without the user
  1350→trait) to approximate causal effects via the LLM. Based on these
  1351→effects, tokens are classified as either preference-driven or not.
  1352→Since the extracted preference-driven tokens are discrete and lack
  1353→smooth semantics, we reconstruct them into coherent, fluent sen-
  1354→tences to

  1359→personalized text generation.
  1360→methods have been proposed: ActAdd [ 48] performs activation
  1361→addition using contrastive-derived steering vectors for controlling
  1362→sentiment and topic, while CAA [ 37] enhances steering precision
  1363→by leveraging mass-mean activation differences.
  1364→Recently, some works [ 5,7] have begun exploring activation
  1365→steering for LLM personalization, focusing on two main imple-
  1366→mentations: influence vector [ 13] and style vector [ 64], which show
  1367→promising potential for adapting model behavior to individual users.
  1368→However, these steering-based methods overlook the fact that user
  1369→data is not entirely produced by the user and that each data piece
  1370→often contains heterogeneous information, leading to suboptimal
  1371→performance in LLM personalization. To the best of our ability, our
  1372→proposed SteerX addresses this limitation.

  1394→the user history (i.e.,inputting 𝑃only). A representative activation
  1395→steering method of this approach is Context Steering (CoS) [ 13],

  1403→during LLM generation:

  1415→PersonaLLM: Investigating the Ability of Large Language Models to
  1416→Express Personality Traits
  1417→Hang Jiang†‡, Xiajie Zhang†, Xubo Cao∔, Cynthia Breazeal†, Deb Roy†‡, Jad Kabbara†‡
  1418→†Massachusetts Institute of Technology,∔Stanford University
  1419→‡MIT Center for Constructive Communication
  1420→{hjian42, xiajie, cynthiab, dkroy, jkabbara}@mit.edu, xcao@stanford.edu
  1421→Abstract
  1422→Despite the many use cases for large language
  1423→models (LLMs) in creating personalized chat-
  1424→bots, there has been limited research on evaluat-
  1425→ing the extent to which the behaviors of person-
  1426→alized LLMs accurately and consistently reflect
  1427→specific personality traits. We consider study-
  1428→ing the behavior of LLM-based agents which
  1429→we refer to as LLM personas and present a
  1430→case study with GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 to investi-
  1431→gate whether LLMs can generate content that
  1432→aligns with their assigned personality profiles.
  1433→To this end, we simulate distinct LLM personas
  1434→based on the Big Five personality model, have
  1435→them complete the 44-item Big Five Inven-
  1436→tory (BFI) personality test and a story writing
  1437→task, and then assess their essays with auto-
  1438→matic and human evaluations. Results show
  1439→thatLLM personas ’ self-reported BFI scores
  1440→are consistent with their designated personality
  1441→types, with large effect sizes observed across
  1442→five traits. Additionally, LLM personas ’ writ-
  1443→ings have emerging representative linguistic
  1444→patterns for personality traits when compared
  1445→with a human writing corpus. Furthermore, hu-
  1446→man evaluation shows that humans can perceive
  1447→some personality traits with an accuracy of up
  1448→to 80%. Interestingly, the accuracy drops sig-
  1449→nificantly when the annotators were informed
  1450→of AI authorship.
  1451→1 Introduction
  1452→With LLMs’ impressive ability to engage in human-
  1453→like conversations, there has been a surge of inter-
  1454→est in building personalized AI agents that inter-
  1455→act with and support humans in various contexts.
  1456→Startups such as Character AI1and Replika2have
  1457→engaged many users through virtual characters on
  1458→their fast-growing platforms. Meanwhile, in the
  1459→academic sphere, research (Park et al., 2023; Wang
  1460→et al., 2023b) has also suggested that generative
  1461→1https://character.ai/
  1462→2https://replika.ai/agents can exhibit believable human behavior and
  1463→could potentially be used to simulate human agents
  1464→in social science studies. However, while these
  1465→generative characters are becoming ubiquitous, it
  1466→is a common yet unsubstantiated assumption that
  1467→these agents consistently behave in a human-like
  1468→manner. Recent studies in the field of LLMs and
  1469→personality have started to provide some empirical
  1470→support. For example, recent research has studied
  1471→personality expression in LLM-generated content
  1472→(Li et al., 2022; Pan and Zeng, 2023; Safdari et al.,
  1473→2023), created new benchmarks to measure per-
  1474→sonality expressed by LLMs (Jiang et al., 2022a;
  1475→Wang et al., 2023a; Mao et al., 2023), and proposed
  1476→better prompting techniques to induce (Karra et al.,
  1477→2022; Jiang et al., 2022a,b; Caron and Srivastava,
  1478→2022; Li et al., 2023), and
  1479→
  1480→--- PAGE 2 ---
  1481→Figure 1: Illustration of the core workflow of the paper. The left section presents the prompts designed to create LLM
  1482→personas . The center section shows the prompt used to instruct models to write stories. The right section outlines
  1483→the three-pronged analytical approach: LIWC analysis, story evaluation, and text-based personality prediction.
  1484→To assess their generated stories, we extract psy-
  1485→cholinguistic features using dictionaries that have
  1486→been designed to analyze human behavior and pro-
  1487→pose to use these features to study the behavior of
  1488→LLM personas . We also investigate whether LLM
  1489→personas are proficient in using lexicons related
  1490→to their assigned personality profiles and whether
  1491→they can convincingly portray these personalities
  1492→to human observers. In other words, can human
  1493→evaluators discern the assigned personality traits
  1494→of the LLM personas based on their narratives?
  1495→Lastly, we suggest potential avenues for extending
  1496→this evaluation to more real-life scenarios, such as
  1497→multi-round dialogues and action planning. Moti-
  1498→vated by these inquiries, we aim to provide a com-
  1499→prehensive evaluation of LLM personas , focusing

1500→on the following research questions (RQs):
  1501→•RQ1 : Can LLMs reflect the behavior of their
  1502→assigned personality profiles when complet-
  1503→ing the Big Five Personality Inventory (BFI)
  1504→assessment?
  1505→•RQ2 : What linguistic patterns are evident in
  1506→the stories generated by LLM personas ?
  1507→•RQ3 : How do humans and LLM raters evalu-
  1508→ate the stories generated by LLM personas ?
  1509→•RQ4 : Can humans and LLMs accurately per-
  1510→ceive the Big Five personality traits from sto-
  1511→ries generated by LLM personas ?2 Experiment Design
  1512→As shown in Figure 1, this paper investigates the
  1513→behavior of LLM personas through a multi-faceted
  1514→approach. We start by creating LLM personas with
  1515→distinct personality traits and administer a person-
  1516→ality assessment to them. Subsequently, we prompt
  1517→these LLM personas to write stories, which we
  1518→then analyze using the widely adopted Linguis-
  1519→tic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC) framework.
  1520→Following this analysis, we recruit human evalu-
  1521→ators to manually evaluate the stories and concur-
  1522→rently carry out an LLM-based automatic evalua-
  1523→tion. Both human and LLM evaluators are required
  1524→to (1) assess these stories across six dimensions,
  1525→namely readability, personalness, redundancy, co-
  1526→hesiveness, likeability, and believability, and (2)
  1527→infer the personality traits assigned to the LLM
  1528→personas from the stories. The code, data, and
  1529→annotations for our experiments are open sourced3.
  1530→2.1 Experiment Setup
  1531→2.1.1 Model Settings
  1532→We conduct the BFI assessment and LIWC
  1533→analysis on state-of-the-art LLMs including
  1534→open-source and closed-source models. We
  1535→include in the main paper the results for
  1536→GPT-3.5 ( GPT-3.5-turbo-0613 ) and GPT-4
  1537→(GPT-4-0613 )4because our results show that they
  1538→3https://github.com/hjian42/PersonaLLM
  1539→4https://platform.openai.com/docs/models
  1540→
  1541→--- PAGE 3 ---
  1542→are more effective at aligning with the designated
  1543→personas. Results for LLaMA-2 are presented
  1544→in Appendix F and Appendix G. Temperature
  1545→is set as 0.7 to introduce variability in personas’
  1546→behavior. All other parameters are kept at their
  1547→default settings.
  1548→2.1.2 LLM Persona Simulation
  1549→For GPT-3.5 and GPT-4, we simulate 10 LLM
  1550→personas for each combination of the binary Big
  1551→Five personality types, resulting in 320 distinct per-
  1552→sonas. They are referred to as GPT-3.5 personas
  1553→andGPT-4 personas respectively. Figure 1 illus-
  1554→trates how we prompt an LLM to generate per-
  1555→sonas and complete specific tasks. Initially, we cre-
  1556→ate an LLM Persona with a system prompt: “You
  1557→are a character who is [TRAIT 1, ..., TRAIT 5].”,
  1558→where [TRAIT 1, ..., TRAIT 5] represents the as-
  1559→signed Big Five personality. For each personality
  1560→dimension, we choose one descriptor among the
  1561→following pairs: (1) extroverted / introverted, (2)
  1562→agreeable / antagonistic, (3) conscientious / uncon-
  1563→scientious, (4) neurotic / emotionally stable, (5)
  1564→open / closed to experience.
  1565→2.1.3 BFI Personality Test
  1566→After specifying a personality type, we ask the LLM
  1567→persona to complete the 44-item Big Five Inven-
  1568→tory (BFI), a widely-used self-report scale designed
  1569→to measure the Big Five personality traits. Only
  1570→the responses that strictly adhere to the instruction
  1571→format “(x) y” are accepted, where (x) indicates
  1572→the question number and y indicates the level of
  1573→agreement on a scale from 1-5. As the green sec-
  1574→tion demonstrates in Figure 1, “(a) 5” would indi-
  1575→cate that the persona strongly agrees that it talks a
  1576→lot. Each LLM persona ’s responses are aggregated
  1577→into five personality scores, which are used in later
  1578→analysis. We use the BFI to assess the personality
  1579→profiles expressed by LLMs because it is widely uti-
  1580→lized in personality-related studies, including many
  1581→studies involving LIWC.
  1582→2.1.4 Storywriting
  1583→Subsequently, we prompt these 320 LLM personas
  1584→to write personal stories with the following sim-
  1585→ple prompt: “Please share a personal story in 800
  1586→words. Do not explicitly mention your personal-
  1587→ity traits in the story.” We impose this restriction
  1588→to prevent the persona from revealing its hidden
  1589→attributes, ensuring an unbiased text-based person-
  1590→ality assessment by other LLMs and human raters.We tried multiple prompt variants in our initial ex-
  1591→periment and decided to purposefully simplify the
  1592→prompt to reduce demand characteristics for the
  1593→generalizability of the result. Examples of LLM-
  1594→generated stories are included in Appendix A.
  1595→2.2 Evaluation Methods
  1596→We evaluate LLM personas ’ storywriting with a
  1597→three-pronged analytical approach. First, we con-
  1598→duct a Linguistic Inquiry and Word Count (LIWC)
  1599→analysis on stories generated by GPT-3.5 and
  1600→GPT-4 personas . Subsequently, we recruit hu-
  1601→man evaluators and use LLM evaluations to rate
  1602→these stories from various perspectives. Lastly, we
  1603→request human evaluators to infer the personality
  1604→traits of the story author. In human evaluation, the

  1612→From 1,000,000 Users to Every User: Scaling Up
  1613→Personalized Preference for User-level Alignment
  1614→Jia-Nan Li1 2∗Jian Guan2∗Songhao Wu1Wei Wu2†Rui Yan1†
  1615→1Gaoling School of Artificial Intelligence, Renmin University of China2Ant Group
  1616→{lijianan, songhaowu, ruiyan}@ruc.edu.cn
  1617→{jianguanthu, wuwei19850318}@gmail.com
  1618→Abstract
  1619→Large language models (LLMs) have traditionally been aligned through one-size-
  1620→fits-all approaches that assume uniform human preferences, fundamentally over-
  1621→looking the diversity in user values and needs. This paper introduces a compre-
  1622→hensive framework for scalable personalized alignment of LLMs. We establish a
  1623→systematic preference space characterizing psychological and behavioral dimen-
  1624→sions, alongside diverse persona representations for robust preference inference
  1625→in real-world scenarios. Building upon this foundation, we introduce ALIGN X,
  1626→a large-scale dataset of over 1.3 million personalized preference examples, and
  1627→develop two complementary alignment approaches: in-context alignment directly
  1628→conditioning on persona representations and preference-bridged alignment mod-
  1629→eling intermediate preference distributions. Extensive experiments demonstrate
  1630→substantial improvements over existing methods, with an average 17.06% accuracy
  1631→gain across four benchmarks while exhibiting a strong adaptation capability to novel
  1632→preferences, robustness to limited user data, and precise preference controllability.
  1633→These results validate our approach toward user-adaptive AI systems.
  1634→1 Introduction
  1635→Alignment with human preference is an essential step in the development of large language models
  1636→(LLMs) [ 1–5]. Current alignment techniques are predominately approached as a one-size-fits-all
  1637→process, by assuming that all humans share the same set of values prescribed by LLM developers [ 6],
  1638→typically characterized by abstract principles such as helpfulness, honesty, and harmlessness [ 7]. The
  1639→monolithic approach fundamentally fails to account for the significant diversity inherent in human
  1640→populations [ 8], encompassing various and often irreconcilable cultural backgrounds, educational
  1641→levels, moral views, and political stands, among others [ 9]. At a time when LLMs serve hundreds of
  1642→millions of users worldwide [ 10], several critical issues have emerged: the systematic exclusion or
  1643→under-representation of minority groups [ 11], reduced user satisfaction and engagement due to the
  1644→lack of personalization [12], etc., as exemplified in Figure 1.
  1645→To address the limitations of existing alignment approaches that rely on oversimplified preference
  1646→dimensions and singular preference direction (e.g., higher helpfulness), we develop a comprehensive
  1647→preference space that captures a wide range of individual variations. Grounded in psychological
  1648→theories of human preferences, we construct this space by synthesizing three complementary sources:
  1649→(1) established psychological models of fundamental needs [ 13–15], (2) preference dimensions
  1650→from contemporary alignment 
  1651→
  1652→--- PAGE 2 ---
  1653→(e.g., Zhihu, REDnote, X, Facebook.3) This integration yields a 90-dimensional preference space with
  1654→flexible directions (i.e., positive ,negative , orneutral ), enabling the representation of an exponential
  1655→space of distinct preference patterns ( 390possible combinations). Having established this space,
  1656→a key challenge emerges: while these preferences could empirically guide response generation
  1657→directly, they typically remain implicit due to privacy concerns and cognitive constraints. Drawing
  1658→from psychological research [ 19,20], we formalize the observable manifestations of preferences
  1659→as personas, capturing both behavioral patterns (e.g., interaction histories) and descriptive features
  1660→(e.g., self-reported profiles). These persona representations enable robust preference inference while
  1661→maintaining practical scalability. We thus formulate personalized alignment as generating preference-
  1662→aligned responses for target prompts given various combinations of persona representations.
  1663→Despite establishing observable persona representations and underlying preference dimensions,
  1664→a key challenge remains: the scarcity of personalized preference data at scale. While existing
  1665→alignment datasets contain feedback from millions of users [ 2], these responses are aggregated and
  1666→anonymous, erasing individual characteristics. Collecting personalized data is challenging due to
  1667→time requirements, privacy concerns, and the need for sufficient samples per user. Previous attempts
  1668→at personalized alignment have been limited to either small-scale studies [21] or synthetic data [22],
  1669→neither adequately capturing real-world preference distributions. To overcome this, we leverage
  1670→large-scale forum data, where multiple responses to the same post naturally reflect diverse preferences.
  1671→We develop a systematic pipeline that transforms forum interactions into structured training data by
  1672→capturing three components: (1) behavioral and descriptive persona representations, (2) preference
  1673→directions, and (3) high-quality posts and preference pairs. This framework yields ALIGN X, a
  1674→large-scale dataset containing over 1.3 million examples of distinct preference patterns.
  1675→Building upon ALIGN X, we propose ALIGN XPERT , the first LLM with large-scale comprehensive
  1676→PERsonalization Training, to the best of our knowledge. ALIGN XPERT employs two complementary
  1677→learning approaches: (1) In-Context Alignment , which incorporates persona representations into
  1678→the context window [ 23] for implicit preference learning, and (2) Preference-Bridged Alignment ,
  1679→which maps personas to structured preference distributions before conditioning response generation,
  1680→enhancing interpretability and control while maintaining robust generalization to diverse user groups.
  1681→Extensive experiments across four personalized alignment benchmarks demonstrate that both
  1682→ALIGN XPERT variants outperform state-of-the-art aligned LLMs [ 24–26] and strong personalization
  1683→baselines [ 27] by 17.06% and 22.40%, respectively, in pr
  1684→

  1716→LLMs + Persona-Plug = Personalized LLMs
  1717→Jiongnan Liu1†, Yutao Zhu1, Shuting Wang1, Xiaochi Wei2
  1718→Erxue Min2, Yu Lu2, Shuaiqiang Wang2, Dawei Yin2, Zhicheng Dou1∗
  1719→1Gaoling School of Artificial Intelligence, Renmin University of China
  1720→2Baidu Inc.
  1721→liujn@ruc.edu.cn, dou@ruc.edu.cn
  1722→Abstract
  1723→Personalization plays a critical role in numer-
  1724→ous language tasks and applications, since users
  1725→with the same requirements may prefer diverse
  1726→outputs based on their individual interests. This
  1727→has led to the development of various person-
  1728→alized approaches aimed at adapting large lan-
  1729→guage models (LLMs) to generate customized
  1730→outputs aligned with user preferences. Some of
  1731→them involve fine-tuning a unique personalized
  1732→LLM for each user, which is too expensive for
  1733→widespread application. Alternative approaches
  1734→introduce personalization information in a plug-
  1735→and-play manner by retrieving the user’s rele-
  1736→vant historical texts as demonstrations. How-
  1737→ever, this retrieval-based strategy may break
  1738→the continuity of the user history and fail to
  1739→capture the user’s overall styles and patterns,
  1740→hence leading to sub-optimal performance. To
  1741→address these challenges, we propose a novel
  1742→personalized LLM model, PPlug . It constructs
  1743→a user-specific embedding for each individual
  1744→by modeling all her historical contexts through
  1745→a lightweight plug-in user embedder module.
  1746→By attaching this embedding to the task input,
  1747→LLMs can better understand and capture user
  1748→habits and preferences, thereby producing more
  1749→personalized outputs without tuning their own
  1750→parameters. Extensive experiments on various
  1751→tasks in the language model personalization
  1752→(LaMP) benchmark demonstrate that the pro-
  1753→posed model significantly outperforms existing
  1754→personalized LLM approaches.
  1755→1 Introduction
  1756→Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated
  1757→extraordinary capabilities in natural language un-
  1758→derstanding, generation, and reasoning (Zhao et al.,
  1759→2023; Brown et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2023; Liu
  1760→et al., 2023), becoming increasingly essential tools
  1761→for assisting with everyday tasks. However, the
  1762→†This work is done during Jiongnan Liu’s internship at
  1763→Baidu Inc.
  1764→*Corresponding author.dominant usage pattern of LLMs follows a one-size-
  1765→fits-all approach, where similar responses are pro-
  1766→vided to different users given the same input. While
  1767→sampling-based decoding strategies can introduce
  1768→some diversity, this approach fails to account for in-
  1769→dividual user preferences, reducing engagement in
  1770→human-machine interactions. This problem is even
  1771→severe in scenarios requiring tailored responses to
  1772→align with subjective user profiles, such as draft-
  1773→ing personalized speeches. Consequently, person-
  1774→alized LLMs have attracted significant interest in
  1775→both industry and academic research (Salemi et al.,
  1776→2024b,a; Zhuang et al., 2024; Kumar et al., 2024;
  1777→Tan et al., 2024b,a; Richardson et al., 2023; Woz-
  1778→niak et al., 2024).
  1779→A straightforward strategy for building person-
  1780→alized LLMs is to fine-tune a specific LLM on in-
  1781→dividual use
  1782→
  1783→--- PAGE 2 ---
  1784→LLM (A)User inputUser historiesUser (A)(a) Tuned MethodsLLM (B)User (B)LLMRetrieved behaviors
  1785→(b) Retrieval-based MethodsLLMUser EmbedderPersonalized embedding(c) Our MethodFigure 1: The comparison of our proposed personalized LLM and previous personalized LLM approaches
  1786→understand the users’ overall styles than to refer to
  1787→specific histories. Unfortunately, the retrieval pro-
  1788→cess typically focuses on relevance to the current
  1789→input rather than identifying deeper user prefer-
  1790→ences embedded in all historical data. As a result,
  1791→the selective utilization of user histories in retrieval-
  1792→based personalized LLMs may disturb the model
  1793→to capture user comprehensive manners and lead to
  1794→sub-optimal performance.
  1795→Therefore, a better strategy for personalizing
  1796→LLMs is to plug the user’s holistic styles into the
  1797→LLMs without modification of their structures and
  1798→parameters. To achieve this, we propose a persona-
  1799→plug ( PPlug ) model. It involves a lightweight plug-
  1800→in user embedder module that embeds user histori-
  1801→cal patterns into a single user-specific embedding
